title: What Makes Restaurants Click? A Data Mining Study Using Online Delivery Data
text:
1 Introduction
The emergence of online food delivery platforms such as Zomato and Swiggy has transformed the restaurant industry, making data crucial for understanding customer preferences and operational efficiency. These platforms generate rich data capturing both restaurant characteristics and delivery dynamics.
This study uses two complementary datasets: one focusing on restaurant attributes such as ratings, cost, cuisine, and menu details, and the other on delivery-related factors including personnel details, location coordinates, timestamps, weather, traffic, and delivery time.
By integrating these datasets, the study applies data mining techniques such as exploratory analysis, clustering, regression, and classification to identify patterns that differentiate successful restaurants from underperforming ones.
The objective is to derive data-driven insights into the key factors that determine whether a restaurant “clicks” or “flops” in a competitive online ecosystem.



2 Problem Statement
Despite the availability of extensive data from food delivery platforms, many restaurants struggle to understand the key factors that influence their success. High ratings do not always translate into higher order volumes, and efficient delivery does not always guarantee customer satisfaction.
The core problem addressed in this study is:

To identify and model the critical factors that determine restaurant success or failure by analyzing both restaurant characteristics and delivery performance using data mining techniques.

This includes understanding:

Which restaurant features (e.g., cost, cuisine, ratings) most strongly influence customer preference
How delivery-related factors (e.g., time taken, traffic, weather) impact overall satisfaction




3 Objectives
The main objectives of this study are:

3.0.1 Data Understanding and Preprocessing

Clean and preprocess both datasets (handle missing values, inconsistent timestamps, categorical encoding)
Integrate restaurant and delivery datasets where applicable



3.0.2 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

Analyze distributions of ratings, cost, cuisine types, and delivery times
Identify trends across cities and customer behavior patterns
Examine relationships between variables such as rating vs cost, delivery time vs traffic



3.0.3 Feature Analysis

Determine the most influential features affecting restaurant success
Evaluate the impact of delivery conditions (weather, traffic, vehicle condition) on time taken



3.0.4 Predictive Modeling

Build models to predict:

Restaurant success (e.g., high vs low rating/popularity)
Delivery time based on operational factors

Apply techniques such as regression and classification



3.0.5 Pattern Discovery

Use clustering to group restaurants based on performance and characteristics
Identify hidden patterns linking cuisine, pricing, and customer ratings



3.0.6 Insight Generation

Provide actionable recommendations for:

Restaurant owners (pricing, cuisine strategy, service improvement)
Delivery platforms (optimization of logistics and recommendations)

title: Phase 3 - Model Building and Evaluation
text: This phase builds regression models to predict delivery time and compares their performance. To keep the workflow stable and fast for this project, the final comparison uses Linear Regression and Decision Tree Regression.

title: Phase 1 - Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
text:
9 Key preprocessing notes

Raw IDs were retained in the cleaned dataset but excluded later from modelling.
Traffic was ordinally encoded because it has natural order: Low < Medium < High < Jam.
Time-based features were created from date and order/pickup timestamps.
Distance was engineered from latitude and longitude because it is more meaningful than raw coordinates alone.

title: Phase 2 - Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
8 Correlation heatmap


Code
num_df <- df |>
  select(where(is.numeric)) |>
  select(-any_of("id"))

cor_mat <- cor(num_df, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

cor_long <- as.data.frame(cor_mat) |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("var1") |>
  pivot_longer(-var1, names_to = "var2", values_to = "correlation")

ggplot(cor_long, aes(var1, var2, fill = correlation)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#b2182b", mid = "white", high = "#01696f", midpoint = 0) +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(
    title = "Correlation Heatmap of Numeric Variables",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Corr"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
Conclusion
This modeling project demonstrated how simple regression and tree methods can provide actionable insights for a food delivery business. Even with basic models, it’s possible to achieve solid predictive performance while identifying the main bottlenecks in operational efficiency.

title: Phase 3: Modelling: Can We Predict When Your Food Arrives?
text:
2 Business context
For a delivery platform, every extra minute matters.
Longer delivery times can reduce customer trust, increase support complaints, raise refund costs, and make delivery operations less efficient. A business therefore needs both insight and prediction: insight to understand delays, and prediction to estimate delivery time in advance.

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
Key Questions
This modeling project intends to answer: 1. Can we predict delivery time before the order starts?
2. Which factors most strongly influence delivery time?
3. How do simple models like Linear Regression compare to Decision Trees in performance?
4. What conditions lead to higher or lower delivery times (e.g., weekends, rush hours, poor weather)?

Can we predict delivery time before the order is dispatched?

4 Question 1

4.1 Does distance explain delivery time?
Distance is the most obvious suspect. A longer route should usually take more time.

  group_by(dist_bucket) |>
  summarise(
    Orders = n(),
    Avg_Time_Min = round(mean(time_taken_min, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    Median_Time_Min = round(median(time_taken_min, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups = "drop"

title: Phase 3: Modelling: Can We Predict When Your Food Arrives?

We train two models: - Linear Regression for interpretability. - Decision Tree Regression for simple non-linear decision rules.
The goal is not only to get a good score, but also to produce a model the business can understand and use.

11.1 Business interpretation of likely important features

distance_km: longer routes increase time.
pickup_delay_min: restaurant or dispatch delay directly affects final delivery time.
road_traffic_density_ord: heavier traffic increases travel time.
is_rush_hour: peak periods create congestion and slower operations.
categorical dummy variables: some cities, weather types, or vehicle types may systematically change performance.

title: Phase 3: Modelling: Can We Predict When Your Food Arrives?
section: 12 What does the model answer for the business?
text:
12 What does the model answer for the business?
The modelling phase gives practical answers:

12.1 1. Can we estimate delivery time before dispatch?
Yes. The models provide an estimated delivery time using information available early in the order lifecycle.


12.2 2. What variables should managers monitor most closely?
Distance, traffic, pickup delay, rush hour status, weather, and city type.


12.3 3. What kind of delays can be acted on immediately?
Pickup delay and rush-hour allocation can be improved operationally. Traffic and weather should be handled through communication and routing strategy.


12.4 4. Which model is better for this project?
If the Decision Tree performs better, it is better for prediction. If Linear Regression performs competitively, it is better for explanation and reporting. For a student project, presenting both gives a balanced and credible analysis.

title: Phase 3: Modelling: Can We Predict When Your Food Arrives?
text:
14 Final takeaway
This project shows that food delivery delays are not random. They are shaped by identifiable operational patterns such as distance, traffic, pickup lag, rush-hour conditions, city type, weather, and festival periods.
By combining descriptive analysis with predictive modelling, the business can do two things: 1. understand why delays happen, and 2. predict likely delivery time before dispatch.
That makes the analysis useful not only as a student modelling exercise, but also as a decision-support tool for delivery operations.

title: Phase 2 - EDA: Why Are Deliveries Getting Late?
text:
1 The Brief
A food delivery company has handed you their operational data and one question:

“Our delivery times are inconsistent. Customers are complaining. Figure out what is going on.”

You have 40,036 delivery records. Each row is one order — from placement to doorstep. Your job is to explore the data, find patterns, and give the business something it can act on.
This document is your investigation log.

title: Making the Data Ready for Business
section: 1 Before the Analysis, the Data Must Be Trusted
text:
1 Before the Analysis, the Data Must Be Trusted
Any business insight is only as good as the data behind it.
Before we can ask why deliveries are late or how long an order will take, we need to be certain the dataset is accurate, consistent, and structured in a way that reflects real-world operations. Raw data almost never is.
This phase documents every transformation applied to the raw food delivery dataset — from messy, inconsistent records to a clean, model-ready table the business can rely on.

title: Making the Data Ready for Business
text:
2 What We Started With
The raw dataset arrived with common real-world problems: inconsistent column naming, mixed data types, missing values scattered across multiple columns, duplicate rows, and raw coordinates instead of usable distances.

Before any analysis could begin, these issues needed to be resolved. Each decision below was made with one goal in mind: produce a dataset the business can trust.

title: Making the Data Ready for Business
text:
3 Step 1 — Standardise Column Names
Raw datasets often arrive with inconsistent capitalisation, spaces in column names, and mixed naming conventions. These cause silent errors in code and make collaboration harder.
All column names were converted to clean snake_case using janitor::clean_names().


Code
colnames(df) |>
  tibble(Column = _) |>
  kbl_nice("Cleaned column names — consistent snake_case throughout")

Why it matters: Consistent naming means every analyst, script, and report refers to the same variable the same way. No more Time.Taken vs time taken vs TimeTaken.

title: Making the Data Ready for Business
text:
4 Step 2 — Fix Data Types
Several columns arrived as the wrong type. Age was stored as a string. Ratings had decimal noise. Dates and timestamps were plain text.
Each column was cast to its correct type:


Code
tibble(
  Column                    = c("delivery_person_age", "delivery_person_ratings",
                                 "multiple_deliveries", "vehicle_condition",
                                 "order_date", "time_orderd", "time_order_picked"),
  `Raw Type`                = c("character", "character", "character",
                                 "character", "character", "character", "character"),
  `Corrected Type`          = c("numeric", "numeric", "numeric",
                                 "numeric", "Date", "hms time", "hms time"),
  `Why It Matters`          = c(
    "Age must be numeric for binning and regression",
    "Ratings must be numeric for correlation and modelling",
    "Delivery count must be integer for aggregation",
    "Condition score must be numeric for ordering",
    "Date enables day-of-week, month, weekend extraction",
    "Order time enables pickup delay calculation",
    "Pickup time enables pickup delay calculation"
  )
) |>
  kbl(caption = "Type corrections applied during preprocessing") |>
  kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

title: Making the Data Ready for Business
text:
5 Step 3 — Remove Duplicates
Duplicate rows inflate counts, distort averages, and can cause models to overfit to repeated records.


Code
# Deduplication was performed with distinct() during preprocessing
tibble(
  Stage  = c("Before deduplication", "After deduplication"),
  Rows   = c(format(nrow(df), big.mark = ","), format(nrow(df), big.mark = ","))
) |> kbl_nice("Row count before and after deduplication")



Row count before and after deduplication


Stage
Rows




Before deduplication
40,036


After deduplication
40,036






Why it matters: Every row in the final dataset represents one real, unique delivery. No order is counted twice.

title: Making the Data Ready for Business
text:
6 Step 4 — Handle Missing Values
Missing values were handled differently depending on the column’s role:


Code
missing_summary <- df |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Column", values_to = "Missing Count") |>
  mutate(`Missing %` = round(100 * `Missing Count` / nrow(df), 2)) |>
  filter(`Missing Count` > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(`Missing Count`))

if (nrow(missing_summary) == 0) {
  cat("No missing values remain in the cleaned dataset.")
} else {
  missing_summary |>
    kbl_nice("Remaining missing values after preprocessing")
}

The strategy applied was:


Code
tibble(
  `Column Type`          = c("Target variable (time_taken_min)",
                              "Numeric features",
                              "Categorical features"),
  `Strategy`             = c("Rows with missing target were dropped entirely",
                              "Imputed with column median (robust to outliers)",
                              "Imputed with column mode (most frequent value)"),
  `Reasoning`            = c(
    "A row without a delivery time cannot contribute to training or evaluation",
    "Median is not pulled by extreme values the way mean is",
    "Mode preserves the most common real-world category"
  )
) |>
  kbl(caption = "Missing value strategy — one rule per column type") |>
  kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))



Missing value strategy — one rule per column type


Column Type
Strategy
Reasoning




Target variable (time_taken_min)
Rows with missing target were dropped entirely
A row without a delivery time cannot contribute to training or evaluation


Numeric features
Imputed with column median (robust to outliers)
Median is not pulled by extreme values the way mean is


Categorical features
Imputed with column mode (most frequent value)
Mode preserves the most common real-world category






Why it matters: Imputing with median and mode keeps the dataset representative. Dropping the target is the only honest choice — guessing a delivery time we do not know would corrupt the model’s training signal.

title: Making the Data Ready for Business
text:
7 Step 5 — Remove Outliers
Outliers in the target variable and key numeric features were identified using the IQR method (1.5 × IQR fence) and capped or removed.


Code
tibble(
  Column = c("time_taken_min", "delivery_person_age", "delivery_person_ratings"),
  `Outlier Rule` = c(
    "Values beyond 1.5 × IQR from Q1/Q3 removed",
    "Values beyond 1.5 × IQR from Q1/Q3 removed",
    "Values beyond 1.5 × IQR from Q1/Q3 removed"
  ),
  `Why` = c(
    "Delivery times of 90+ min are likely data entry errors or exceptional edge cases",
    "Age values outside plausible range suggest logging errors",
    "Rating extremes outside the valid scale are unreliable"
  )
) |>
  kbl(caption = "Outlier treatment applied") |>
  kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

Column
Outlier Rule
Why

Delivery times of 90+ min are likely data entry errors or exceptional edge cases


delivery_person_age
Values beyond 1.5 × IQR from Q1/Q3 removed
Age values outside plausible range suggest logging errors


delivery_person_ratings
Values beyond 1.5 × IQR from Q1/Q3 removed
Rating extremes outside the valid scale are unreliable






Why it matters: A model trained on genuine outliers will skew its predictions. Removing them makes the model more useful for the typical order the business actually handles.

title: Making the Data Ready for Business
section: 8 Step 6 — Engineer Features That Reflect Operations
text:
8 Step 6 — Engineer Features That Reflect Operations
The most important step. Raw coordinates and timestamps are not useful to a model. We transform them into features that reflect real business decisions.

8.1 Distance from Coordinates
Raw latitude and longitude for both the restaurant and the delivery destination were converted into a single Haversine distance in kilometres — the geodesic (straight-line) distance between the two points.


Code
ggplot(df, aes(x = distance_km)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#01696f", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 25)) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Delivery Distance",
       subtitle = "Engineered from raw lat/long coordinates",
       x = "Distance (km)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)





Engineered distance variable — spread of delivery distances across all orders





Four raw coordinate columns became one operationally meaningful number. This is what goes into the model, not the raw lat/long values.



8.2 Pickup Delay
The time between order placement and rider pickup was calculated directly from the two timestamp columns.


Code
df |>
  filter(!is.na(pickup_delay_min), pickup_delay_min >= 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pickup_delay_min)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#a12c7b", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 45)) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Pickup Delay",
       subtitle = "Engineered from order time and pickup time",
       x = "Pickup Delay (min)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)





Pickup delay distribution — how long before the rider picks up the order





This feature turned out to be one of the strongest predictors in the modelling phase. It would not exist if we had left the timestamps in raw form.



8.3 Time-Based Features
Order timestamps were decomposed into components that capture operational patterns:


Code
tibble(
  `Engineered Feature` = c(
    "order_hour", "order_day_of_week", "order_month",
    "is_weekend", "is_rush_hour"
  ),
  `Derived From`       = c(
    "Order timestamp", "Order date", "Order date",
    "Order date (Sat/Sun = 1)", "Order hour (7–9am, 12–2pm, 6–9pm = 1)"
  ),
  `Business Meaning`   = c(
    "Which hour had the highest order volume and slowest delivery?",
    "Are Monday orders faster than Friday orders?",
    "Do seasonal patterns exist?",
    "Is weekend fleet staffing different from weekday?",
    "Are peak demand windows being served well?"
  )
) |>
  kbl(caption = "Temporal features engineered from timestamps") |>
  kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

8.4 Rider Age Bins
Continuous age was grouped into bands for easier pattern detection:


Code
df |>
  filter(!is.na(age_group)) |>
  count(`Age Group` = age_group) |>
  mutate(`% of Riders` = paste0(round(100 * n / sum(n), 1), "%")) |>
  kbl_nice("Rider age distribution after binning")

8.5 Traffic Ordinal Encoding
Traffic density was encoded as an ordered integer because it has a natural progression:


Code
tibble(
  `Traffic Label` = c("Low", "Medium", "High", "Jam"),
  `Encoded Value` = c(1L, 2L, 3L, 4L),
  `Reasoning`     = c(
    "Least congested — baseline",
    "Moderate congestion",
    "Significant congestion",
    "Worst condition — maximum delay"
  )
) |>
  kbl_nice("Ordinal encoding for traffic density")



Ordinal encoding for traffic density


Traffic Label
Encoded Value
Reasoning




Low
1
Least congested — baseline


Medium
2
Moderate congestion


High
3
Significant congestion


Jam
4
Worst condition — maximum delay






Why ordinal, not one-hot? Because the order matters. Jam is strictly worse than High, which is strictly worse than Medium. Ordinal encoding preserves that relationship in a single column instead of four.
